title: References
description: Create alias names for existing variables.

slides.title: A Second Name 👔
slides.content:
A **reference variable** is a "reference" (an alias) to an already-existing variable.

It allows you to use two different variable names to interact with the exact same piece of data.

slides.title: Defining References
slides.content: To turn a variable into a reference alias, you must attach the `&` symbol to its data type.
slides.codeSnippet:
string food = "Pizza";  // Standard food variable
string &meal = food;    // meal is now a reference alias to food

cout << food << "\n"; // Prints Pizza
cout << meal << "\n"; // Prints Pizza

quizData.question: In the example above, does modifying `meal` modify `food`?

options.text: Yes, because they point to the exact same physical memory address

quizData.explanation: Because `meal` is a reference (`&`), it perfectly shadows the `food` variable. They are one and the same item.

slides.title: Efficiency 🚀
slides.content: Often, developers pass huge datasets as references to functions to prevent the computer from duplicating megabytes or gigabytes of RAM dynamically when handing data off.

quizData.question: To create a reference variable tied to a `double x`, you would type `double &y = x;`
quizData.explanation: Yes, adding the `&` sign to the datatype implies that the variable you are making is an alias.
